12 min read + videos
In Part 2 we’re going to cover how to set up the automation that
will power your SPPs.
There are many ways to create an SPP along with the supporting
automation, so we’re going to show three versions, each a little
different. Through this lens, you’ll learn the building blocks that
will equip you to build an expression of SPP that �ts your needs
and time resources.
▪ Basic SPP (single SPP in the Stack)
▪ Upgraded Basic SPP (single SPPs in the Stack)
▪ Workhorse SPP (three SPPs in the Stack)
Once you know how to set up a single stack SPP, how to upgrade
it, and understand how to build a multi-SPP Stack, you’ll have
everything you need to construct any size SPP Stack.
One more thing before we get started.
There are hundreds of ESPs (email service providers), and we can’t
Story-Powered Promotions
(SPPs) — Part 2
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 30 --

cover every variation and nuance. We use ConvertKit. Having
used a handful of ESPs over the past two decades, we can say
without hesitation, that ConvertKit represents what we refer to as
an expression of ‘elegant simplicity.’
It’s a perfect balance of useful features which are easily accessible,
minus the bloat of some of the larger ESPs, trying to be all things
to all marketers.
“I would not give a �g for the simplicity this side of complexity; but I
would give my life for the simplicity the other side of complexity.” —
Oliver Wendell Holmes, U.S. Supreme Court Justice
We’ll use ConvertKit to demonstrate how to set up the
automations that power SPPs. It’ll be relatively easy to model the
set up in any decent ESP on the market. If the ESP you use doesn’t
support this level of relatively basic automation, consider
jumping ship.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 30 --

For context, let’s start by unpacking a basic set up, then expand to
where we need to get to from there.
Simplistic
The most basic version of an SPP, is a simple follow-up sequence
with a link trigger that subscribes someone (Called a ‘Sequence’ in
CK).
Someone clicks a link to indicate active intent, and they’re
subscribed, at which point they receive the sequence. Simple. This
is exactly how we set up an SOS. No need for fancy automation.
However, there are two problems with this simplistic version of a
SPP, which is why we don’t recommend you set an SPP in the
same way we do for an SOS.
�. What happens if/when subscriber 1234 clicks the link again
— or another link trigger for the same SPP — an hour later,
day later, month later, year later?
�. CK, and most other ESPs will give you the option of
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 30 --

preventing a subscriber from re-subscribing to a sequence,
or it’ll reset them, sending them through again (starting
from email #1).
Neither of these are optimal scenarios.
Clicking a link only for nothing to happen (say they had been
through the sequence six months earlier), would be frustrating.
Or for a sequence to reset, and the exact same emails resent,
perhaps just as bad.
The best automation doesn’t look like automation. It feels organic
and personal, not a dumb machine sending the same stuff.
For this reason, we always set up a baseline automation to control
every SPP, even if you only have a single SPP.
The same automation can handle a single SPP, or multiple SPPs.
As you write a new one, you add it to the automation, and you’re
all set.
For context, let’s look at this hypothetical scenario which we
shared in a previous module.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 30 --

From this illustration, it’s easy to think of an SPP as the same as
an SOS, but with the purpose to promote an offer. But this is not
the case.
To demonstrate this point, let’s modify the above scenario slightly
(see bottom-right).
It’s the SAME potential lead �ow, but now extended. SPP (A) can
represent any product offer — but we’ll use AoE because it’s a
frame of reference we all know. SPP (A) is a promotion for AoE.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 30 --

Now look at the last VNL (far right). Maybe a prospect doesn’t
respond to the link in the previous VNL, which leads to the SOS.
But she clicks the Hard Bridge link in the P.S. area of the last VNL.
But it’s the same SPP!
In this scenario you wouldn’t want to send the exact same SPP to
her, right? So we need a “smart” mechanism to account for this
eventuality.
This is why every SPP is controlled by a higher-order automation,
that will make sure that an SPP will never be sent to the same
person twice (unless you want it to).
Remember, SPPs are reusable assets. We create them, add them to
a “stack,” and leave it to the automation to manage.
Before we look at this, it’ll be helpful to zoom out and look at a
wide angle perspective, and talk about SPP Stacks.
SPP Stacks
We’re going to build three versions of an SPP Stack, and by
observing these, you’ll have everything you need to build an SPP
Stack that matches your needs of any size.
To reiterate, unlike with a Soap Opera Series, which is only a
simple Sequence (in CK), an SPP needs a controlling automation
to add the functionality we need.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 30 --

Zoomed out, here are two single-stack SPPs.
(The yellow dot under the tag trigger represents the automation that
controls all SPPs within the stack.)
We will always recommend you build an “upgraded” SPP Stack —
regardless of size — even when it’s only to control a single SPP.
Now let’s look at much larger SPP Stack, each with three SPPs.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 30 --

Each SPP Stack is triggered by the same link trigger (which we’ll
cover in Part 3), which applies a tag, and triggers the automation
for that Stack.
For us, as an example — SPP Stack A could be for Art of Email, SPP
Stack B for Sphere of In�uence, SPP Stack C for The Traf�c
Engine, and SPP Stack D for The Durable Business.
Within each stack can be any number of SPPs for that product.
Our automation (yellow dot in the image), will automatically send
a subscriber the �rst SPP they’ve not received before.
If it’s their �rst time in SPP Stack A, for example, they’ll receive
SPP A1. If they received SPP A1 the last time they triggered this
SPP, they’ll receive SPP A2, etc. You can keep adding new SPPs to a
Stack over time, expanding your opportunities to convert
someone from prospect to customer for a product. With that said,
there’s a point of diminishing returns, hence the SPP (VIP), an
optional SPP we’ll cover later.
Before we build the three versions of an SPP Stack, let’s look at the
ingredients we need for construction.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 30 --

Ingredients of an SPP
Outside of writing an SPP, there are a list of prerequisite
ingredients you’ll need to bake your SPP cake.
▪ One Sequence per SPP (In Drip it’s called a Campaign,
AWeber a Follow-Up, etc.)
▪ Appropriate Tags
▪ Appropriate Custom Fields
▪ Appropriate Rules (for Link Triggers)
▪ Automation (one per SPP Stack)
SPP Stack Tags & Custom Fields
Tags are used by ESPs to trigger simple ‘if this then that’-style
rules and more complex automations, and custom �elds to track
state changes.
For this reason, having a robust tagging taxonomy or style guide
will serve you well. God knows, this stuff can easily get out of
control when tag naming is made up on the �y.
Tags:
Taxonomy: ACTION - NAME - META
ACTION is something that’s happened, like: Triggered, Interested,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 30 --

Purchased, Muted, Flagged, etc.
NAME is what it is: product, course, campaign name, etc.
META is anything extra that can identify a different version of
NAME, or when (date), or what for (Black Friday), etc.
Examples:
▪ 	SPP - AoE
▪ 	SPP - TTE
▪ 	Interested - AoE - Oct 2021
▪ 	Purchased - AoE
▪ 	Purchased - AoE Workshop - Live Cohort
▪ 	Purchased - AoE Workshop - Evergreen
▪ 	Enrolled - Emergent Marketing Series - RBS
▪ 	Internal - TDB -- add to new COS
▪ 	Waiting List - SOI - Version 2
▪ 	Refunded - AoE
▪ Etc.
Keep in mind that tags are typically sorted in alphabetical order
(they are in ConvertKit), like this:
▪ 	Purchased - AoE
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 30 --

▪ 	Purchased - AoE Workshop
▪ 	Purchased - TTE
▪ 	Purchased - SOI
▪ 	Purchased - TDB
▪ 	Purchased - MBW-LE
For this reason, you may prefer to group NAMES instead of ACTION ,
in which case you should swap ACTION and NAME around, which
would then list your tags like this:
▪ 	AoE - Interested - PLS (Oct 2021)
▪ 	AoE - Muted - PLS (Oct 2021)
▪ 	AoE - Purchased
▪ 	AoE - Refunded
▪ 	TTE - Interested - PLS (Fall 2020)
▪ 	TTE - Muted - PLS (Fall 2020)
▪ 	TTE - Purchased
▪ 	TTE - Refunded
Pick the taxonomy that works for you. There is no right or wrong
method, unless you change your naming convention along the
way, or have no system at all. Pick one, then stick with it.
Note (worth mentioning): Some ESPs make it easy to rename tags,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 30 --

and some do not (without terrible implications). With CK, ‘pretty
names’ are not used by the underlying codebase (IDs are), it’s just for
the humans. So we can rename a tag, and all uses and references of
that tag will be changed/updated, causing no harm. When we used
Drip, renaming a tag would break all rules and automations
associated with said tag. Nightmare! Perhaps this has changed. But
the point is, the best time to create a naming convention is in the
beginning.
Custom Fields:
For the purposes of SPPs, we use custom �elds to track state
changes (example: which, if any, SPP has someone been through).
More broadly, custom �elds can be used for segmentation, to
identify purchase details, and other uses cases outside of the
scope of this module.
When using custom �elds, sticking to these “rules” will,
regardless of your ESP, avoid any potential downstream issues:
▪ No spaces. Use underscores.
▪ All lowercase.
▪ No special characters.
▪ The �rst character shouldn’t be a number.
Examples:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 30 --

▪ 	spp_aoe
▪ 	spp_tte
▪ 	spp_tdb
▪ 	spp_soi
We use a custom �eld to indicate the SPP status for a subscriber.
The value will be blank if they’ve never triggered the SPP before,
or it’ll contain the value of which SPP they’ve completed.
How to Build an SPP Stack (Basic,
Upgraded, and Workhorse)
We’ll start with the most basic SPP Stack containing a single SPP.
Then we’ll “upgrade” it to include a SPP VIP element. Finally, we’ll
construct a full SPP Stack with three SPPs and an SPP VIP
element.
Basic SPP Stack
The order in which we build an SPP Stack is:
�. create appropriate tags and custom �elds,
�. create all the sequences you’ll need,
�. create automation (last).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 30 --

Once the appropriate tags and custom �elds have been created, it’s
time to create an SPP Sequence.
Dashboard Send Sequences
Next, we can build the automation �ow that will control this basic
SPP Stack. This automation can be modeled as a template. If you
already use ConvertKit, you can copy this directly to your account
here (please do not share this automation in public).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 30 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 30 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 30 --

When you look at the automation, what becomes obvious is that
it’s constructed in such a way that someone can only receive the
SPP once. This is by design, as already mentioned. Of course, if
you did want people to be able go through it multiple times, it’s an
easy change.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 30 --

However, we suggest you rather upgrade this SPP Stack to include
one more special SPP, which we call an SPP VIP.
This is a single email that communicates a speci�c message to
someone (based on the context they’re receiving it). This message
will be different based on the size of your SPP Stack. At the most
basic level, it’ll prevent:
�. nothing from happening (e.g. when someone triggers a
single SPP Stack which they have already been through),
�. or from looping someone back through the same SPP
(multiple times).
A SPP VIP message can be as simple as this:
Subject: Houston, we have a problem!
Hey FNAME,
Whoops, hold on there, tiger!
Looks like you’ve already been sent this promotion before. So I want to
make 100% sure you would like to receive it again.
If so, click here, and I’ll send it to you.
Hit me up anytime if you have a speci�c question about this PRODUCT,
okay?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 30 --

This is a respectful way of adding meaningful nuance to your
email system. Let’s add one of these now.
Adding an SPP VIP
First we need to create a Link Trigger that’ll “reset” the spp_aoe
(or whatever your SPP Stack custom �eld is named) custom �eld,
which will allow the SPP to be resent to said subscriber.
Here’s an example of his. All Link Triggers need to have a
destination URL. Unlike in the example below, yours should go to
a landing page con�rming you’re just resent the promotion, and
they should go check their inbox.
Next we rewrite the appropriate custom �eld, spp_aoe in this
example, with a value of reset that will trigger a resend
automation.
(Swap out reset with whatever makes sense for you.)
Best, YOUR-NAME
www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 30 --

… which triggers this automation:
The automation then “blanks” the custom �eld (which can’t be
done directly from a Rule in CK) — allowing said subscriber to be
resent the SPP they’ve already been through — and then adds the
trigger tag — SPP AoE in this example — which will trigger, and
add the subscriber back into the SPP Stack for AoE in our example.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 30 --

Once this Rule and Automation is set up, we can create the “VIP”
sequence. Same as before — Dashboard Send Sequences — create a
new Sequence for your SPP Stack, like the example below.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 21 of 30 --

Next, allow the sequence to be resent. In ConvertKit, this is
disabled by default. Set it to Yes, then save. Do the same for your
SPP sequence that you would like to be resent to subscribers —
SPP - AoE - 1 in our example.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 22 of 30 --

… and now we’re all set to “upgrade” our Basic SPP Stack to this
enhanced version.
The only addition to the previous automation, is that now, instead
of the automation just ending, we send the SPP - AoE - VIP
sequence to a subscriber who does not have a blank custom �eld:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 23 of 30 --

The VIP email will be sent, giving the subscriber the option of
receiving the same SPP again.
Simple.
Okay, now with those building block out of the way, let’s build the
big SPP Stack.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 24 of 30 --

Whether you have one, two, or three SPPs to set up as a Stack, you
don’t need to have already written your SPP sequence(s).
However, in order to set up the SPP Stack automation, you do
need to at least create blank sequences. Once the automation is set
up, you can add the emails to the sequence(s) at any point before
putting it live.
Example: If we were to create a three SPP Stack for AoE, we create
the empty sequence containers �rst (with the �rst email set to no
delay and published), like this:
Here are the blank containers for three SPPs within the AoE SPP
Stack.
SPP Stack Automation
Let’s look at an SPP Stack for AoE that has three SPPs. This
www.getwsodo.com

-- 25 of 30 --

automation can be modeled as a template. If you already use
ConvertKit, you can copy this to your account here (please do not
share this automation in public).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 26 of 30 --

Tags (T), Custom Fields (CF) & Sequences (S) used in this SPP
Stack
▪ (T) SPP - AoE
▪ (T) Purchased - AoE
▪ (T) Flag - SPP - AoE - Complete
▪ (CF) spp_aoe
▪ (S) SPP - AoE - 1
▪ (S) SPP - AoE - 2
▪ (S) SPP - AoE - 3
▪ (S) SPP - AoE - VIP
www.getwsodo.com

-- 27 of 30 --

Now let’s look at the SPP VIP for a SPP Stack made up of 3 or 4
SPPs.
SPP VIP (for 3 or 4 SPP Stacks)
The �rst thing we need to consider, is why has someone been
exposed to 3 (or 4) SPPs for a particular product, yet has not
purchased? Context matters.
They’ve already indicated their active intent to receive every
single SPP for said product. However, there remains a mismatch
between our offer, the themes and angles we’re using, and the
motivation, needs or desire of this prospect.
There are many ways to approach solving this problem, and we’re
not going to prescribe any as “best” to use.
Again: context matters, and your context will differ from ours.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 28 of 30 --

That said, let’s explore a few options:
This is about rewarding someone as a feedback loop, not for their
unwillingness to purchase. It’s about discovering what the
“disconnect” between their needs and our ability to serve those
needs.
Offering a 50% coupon seem like a fair trade in this context.
At this point, we suggest manually reaching out to the prospect in
Subject: FNAME, can we jump on a call?
Hey FNAME,
I would love to jump on a Zoom or Skype call with you and see whether we
can help.
At four separate times, you’ve requested that I send you a promotion for
PRODUCT, yet you’re still not a customer.
If you’re open to chatting with me, I would love to try and discover where
the disconnect is between your needs and my value proposition.
To thank you for your time, I’ll give you a 50% coupon you can use for any
of my products and redeem it anytime. It won’t expire, so no pressure.
Best, YOUR-NAME
www.getwsodo.com

-- 29 of 30 --

question from a ‘regular’ email account (not an email created by
our ESP). We want this email to be (and feel) personal to the
recipient.
We use this “VIP” idea in other creative ways which we’ll discuss
in Module 11: Advanced Ideas.
This lesson my be overwhelming if you’re less technical inclined.
We’re happy to create additional videos if we need a need. Please
ask questions and leave comments below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 30 of 30 --
